title: 怪钟疑案
summary:
During a visit, Poirot explains to Colin that he has been seeking intellectual stimulation through research and problem-solving. He recounts a minor mystery involving three dry orange peels found on his umbrella stand, which he solved by discovering that a new cleaning lady had violated rules by bringing her child to work. Poirot also mentions applying his methods to real-life unsolved mysteries, such as the Charles Bravo case and the case of Constance Kent, claiming he found clues to the motives and culprits.

Turning to literature, Poirot critiques various crime novels. He praises "The Case of the Living Dummy" for its atmosphere and character studies, and "The Adventures of Arsenè Lupin" for its vitality and humor. He describes "The Secret of the Yellow Room" as a masterpiece of logical reasoning. Regarding contemporary authors, he criticizes Ariadne Oliver’s early works for being unrealistic and her portrayal of Finnish characters as inaccurate, though he notes her later improvement in describing police procedures. He discusses Cyril Quin, noting that while the plots are repetitive and revolve around intricate alibis like train and bus schedules, he enjoys unraveling them. In contrast, he dismisses horror novelist Gary Gregson for creating chaotic, unbelievable plots. 

Discussing American writers, Poirot praises Florence Eckle for her methodical pacing but criticizes the frequent, unnecessary inclusion of alcohol consumption in American crime fiction. While he dismisses the hardboiled genre's focus on violence, he concludes that American crime novels are often more cunning and imaginative than British ones, citing Louisa O'Malley as an example.
